item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see much potential in this skip, but it have a lot of issues for me right now. It is a good project for the Oineiroi Hub, but is lacking as a SCP.

1- Technobable isn't clinical tone. Though it helps to set the theme on more fantastic Sci-Fi, on the style of the Foundation it sounds just like lazy writing.

2- On a general note, assuming that something doesn't need to be explained because the Foundation would know is even lazier. SCPs are written on the assumption that you need to explain everything to the guy who suddenly needs the information to do the job.

3- You put too much focus on the Unconscious Real Estate of the guy, and too few on the anomaly. It is an anomaly being contained by the Foundation. The anomaly should be the focus. I see no reason whatsoever for the Foundation to even care about what happens at his mind, it doesn't seem to relate enough with the actual phenomenon going on.

At the end, the anomaly seems just like an excuse to show up the Foundation suddenly interested in claiming territory at OW just for the sake of it, and coming at war with Qi Shao. It isn't at all bad for a tale, or a GOI article. But as an SCP it doesn't make it for me, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A-okay with me. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
– hide block

The following crosslink proposal is being made based on other articles relating to sleep and/or dream related worlds. Articles were screened by the author ahead of time.

Editing this:

The bridge of the vessel representing SCP-2603's subconscious is to be permanently shielded from entry by foreign entities as detailed in security protocol 2603-Prc/Onr-Atlantik:v1.24.

To this:

The bridge of the vessel representing SCP-2603's subconscious is to be permanently shielded from entry by foreign entities as detailed in security protocol 2603-Prc/Onr-Atlantik:v1.24.1

Footnotes
1. Rem/CHOIT1/20170415: Techniques and methods developed and used in containment of SCP-2603 are currently being screened for adaptibility for use with projects 1230 and 2840. Possible uses include an attempt to assess whether or not the class Lamba entities involved are in fact baseline human entities employing similar techniques as used by Omicron Rho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why a post complaining about this not having enough explanation on how MTF enters SCP's dream only got a harsh response - I do understand it could be meaningless to apply real world science to a fictional sci-fi article but the techniques used by MTF should be well known to SCP foundation, and essentially, us. The hostile entities, yes, I don't really care how they enter the subconscious, but there should be at least a sentence, or a phrase about how MTF can manage to explore the dream. I don't know, maybe a technique developed with usage of SCP-XXXX, or the MTFs are made up of psychics.. I'm not saying MTFs should use a real world science, I'm saying it deserves more detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, I liked this quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As an example of the kind of anomaly that Omicron-Rho deals with, this works, and the little world-building detail drops are good, but ultimately this only seems peripherally connected to the Xiapania storyline unless I am missing something, and I wonder if this would have worked better if it was more connected. Upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks. :)

As for the nitpicks:

The repetition of members twice in this sentence comes off as a little awkward, is this a typo?

Yes. Yes it is.

May want to put in parenthesis what the local time for this skip currently is, after all, I would hesitate to call such a detail redundant when it is crucial to containment.

Much like blackboxing the site designation, I shouldn't have to tell the people containing the scip what their local time is. They have company watches you know. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said when I looked this over as a draft, this is neat. Your description of the dream space is quite vivid and the concept of entering a room and being segued to another part of 2603's consciousness is quite interesting. Additionally, Wilson's abilities are pretty cool. +1

+ Time for the Nitpicks!
- Oh no! Not the Nitpicks!

Any attempts to enter SCP-2603's subconscious should be coordinated with the current Senior Geopolitical Analyst attached to this project, and should only be performed by members of NCI-IV certified members of Mobile Task Force Omicron-Rho.

The repetition of members twice in this sentence comes off as a little awkward, is this a typo?

SCP-2603 is to remain awake from 06:00 to 00:00 local time.

May want to put in parenthesis what the local time for this skip currently is, after all, I would hesitate to call such a detail redundant when it is crucial to containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Teleporting
2) Magic

I love this website, its just that this article has very little explaining what is going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's right. Dreams, go figure. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All entrances above deck.

So is there no way to get from below deck to above deck (that is, from the insides of the ship to the "surface" of the ship)? Or can you go from below deck to above deck, but if you turn around and try to go back through the door you'll wind up at the bridge?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, not a complaint and not saying you should change this, but as a technical term, noncognitive doesn't make a whole lot of sense. (See collapsible)

+ show block
– hide block

Cognition is more about the whole structure and action of thought (e.g., conscious or unconscious decision-making, explicit reasoning, learning, memory, etc.), rather than just indicating conscious thought. So cognitive as an adjective is just kind of… broad, only obliquely related to your context, and not giving the exact relationship you intend… I guess.

I'm not aware of a specific term for dream-space in experimental psychology (the interpretation of dreams being a Freudian thing, which is most often to say arbitrary and not evidence-driven), but I know the term unconscious is still in quite common use in various meanings. Full disclosure, though, sleep research is well outside of my specialty (but I am very much in cognitive psych.)

All in all, it wouldn't sound as cool, but nonconscious would be much closer to your meaning.


Still, the made-up term is nifty, so again, not complaining.

I think if I have any complaint about the piece, it's that the physical manifestation portion of the fellow's anomaly is boring and feels pretty tacked on to the much more interesting dream narrative. I think the description was also somewhat difficult to understand, but that's partially because it's such a vague, mushy power. Trying to piece together the whole Philadelphia Experiment bit took some effort.

In any case, very entertaining and well done overall. I'm extremely enjoying your group's content for this contest (and I love the tale tie-in), and look forward to more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a mistake on your part. Between treating this guy as a warzone (Arnold Fitzwilliams comparisons definitely came to mind), incursions into his subconscious, and a freaking MTF member who can shape dreams (why the hell isn't she in containment?), I found myself with a lot of disparate dots and no way to connect them. Far too much is left out and assumed, and it just makes the article confusing.

Plus, if he only converts inorganic matter, why not keep him in a wooden containment chamber?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mixture of footnotes and bibliographic citations causes 1 to be repeated.

It does. Nothing to be done about that without foregoing Wikidot syntax unfortunately.

What exactly is the "noncognitive space"? From context, it doesn't seem to be the dream construct, but it's not clear what else it could be.

SCP-2603's subconscious.

1) If all entrances lead to the ship's bridge, how does anyone get to the other rooms on the ship?

All entrances above deck. I can see how that's not immediately clear from the sentence in question. I'll see what I can do about it. :)

2) If all entrances lead to the bridge, wouldn't the easiest way of defending the bridge be to have some members of the MTF camp out inside the bridge?

It would, but we can't spare MTF members permanently assigned to that post. Which is why it needs to be better shielded some other way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mixture of footnotes and bibliographic citations causes 1 to be repeated.

SCP-2603's noncognitive space

What exactly is the "noncognitive space"? From context, it doesn't seem to be the dream construct, but it's not clear what else it could be.

Unlike most vessels, its interior space is taken up by a variable number of identical decks filled with rooms,

Although structures can be seen above deck, all entrances lead to the ship's bridge: a small featureless room containing only a ship's wheel.

1) If all entrances lead to the ship's bridge, how does anyone get to the other rooms on the ship?

2) If all entrances lead to the bridge, wouldn't the easiest way of defending the bridge be to have some members of the MTF camp out inside the bridge?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like this. Unlike others, I don't have a problem with not detailing how they get into this person's mind; getting into his subconscious is so rote and formulaic for the Foundation, it's not even worth spelling out.

Dream warfare is really cool, and I like how dry and dispassionate the recounting of this clearly absurd and impossible battle was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The criticism. I still liked the article, but that part did throw me a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you agreeing with the criticism or the record scratching? ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My one nitpick is to agree that the "how do they access his dreams" makes a mildly suspension-breaking non-sequitur. Doesn't need to be a full explanation, mind you- a brief mention that the MTF specializes in "psychological reconnaissance" or something would be enough to establish "this is a thing they do via ████████████ and not the result of something SCP-2603 does".

I get that, and the other contest entries for our team will likely give you more details on how that works, but I felt it had no place in a specific SCP article. Can't add it now anyway due to contest rules, but your criticism is and might be addressed afterwards. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the description of the various aspects of dreaming. I actually thought that what was left out was easy enough to guess or leave for explicit explanation later. The stuff with the MTF, especially, was a great example of storytelling through implication.

This is another example that gets me excited for things to come. Good job. +1

EDIT: Mmmmm. I really hate to do this, but now that posting is over, this article just represents a big question mark. The mystery almost seems perfectly set up to be explained later, but it never is. As a prelude to more story, it was great. But as a plot thread that never got picked up or mentioned ever again… I have to downvote for the context here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. At first, I thought that this was all taking place in the real world, not in dream-space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation currently considers SCP-2603 a contested sovereign body, with unknown parties in a dispute concerning the ownership of SCP-2603's noncognitive space.

This is one of the better *cue record scratch* lines I've read lately. Love this, +1.

My one nitpick is to agree that the "how do they access his dreams" makes a mildly suspension-breaking non-sequitur. Doesn't need to be a full explanation, mind you- a brief mention that the MTF specializes in "psychological reconnaissance" or something would be enough to establish "this is a thing they do via ████████████ and not the result of something SCP-2603 does".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The descriptions in the interview, and the way they're just casually played off as normal, were really great. The article on the whole is a super cool sort of kind of colder than usual look at Oneiroi shenanigans. I really enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does SCP-173 move without having joints?
How exactly does a stone alter a mirror so it becomes it portal in SCP-093?

If you're going to dislike articles for not giving you all the answers, you're going to be in a sour mood on this website. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do they get inside the dream? How is it a "war zone" if no real people are in it, just his thoughts? Its all cryptic, with no real answers. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see the potential parallels with SCP-1761, but they really aren't much alike. Basically, what this article (and the rest of the Oneiroi articles/tales) are positing is that everyone is prima real estate. This particular real estate is just in high demand apparently.

As for the exposition needed to tell you, the reader, things that in-universe the Foundation knows from previous dealings with the Oneiroi and from MTF-Omicron-Rho's records, I felt that was something that would weigh down the actual article. I assumed readers would associate the bridge with control, and would get the implication that entities attempting to reach that bridge were vying for control of 2603's subconscious. You're right, it's never spelled out why this is, but I didn't feel it was strictly necessary to make the scip interesting. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly an interesting concept but the execution leaves a little to be desired. The nature of SCP-2603 being a person and also a war zone reminds me strongly of The Republic of Arnold Fitzwilliams, and the description of the dream sequence was terrific.

However, there's no real explanation why the Foundation assumes that the entities are trying to seize control of Tullo's subconsciousness, nor is there an explanation for how they know this can be done by reaching the bridge.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is the SCP entry for The Dream Team in the 2016 MTF Contest. Thanks go out to SoullessSingularity, Faminepulse, Jacob Conwell and Kalinin for their feedback and ass-kickings where necessary.

Thanks for reading. :)

Edit: they are Easter Eggs in there, kudos if you find them. :)
